w:r.w:t: Greetings from the Department of Physics and Astronomy.
have happened in our department over the past year and I will give you a quick highlight.
The Department is delighted to welcome four new faculty members, three of whom
joined in the fall of 2024 and one who will join us in January 2025.
Assistant Professor Pontus Laurell received his PhD in theoretical condensed-matter physics from the University of Texas–Austin and did postdoctoral work at Oak Ridge National
His research focus is on quantum information in condensed matter
systems. Assistant Teaching Professor Samual Roland received his PhD from the University of Michigan in theoretical high energy physics. Subsequently he worked in data science at
Broadcom Inc. and then taught at Stephens College.
Sam is focusing his work on our large enrollment physics and astronomy courses.
Assistant Professor Charles Steinhardt received his PhD in Astronomy from Harvard University, and he comes to us from the University of Copenhagen where he was associate professor in the

His research interests center around galactic assembly and high-redshift astrophysics. Dr. Pavlo Sukhachov will join us in January 2025 as the George H. Vineyard Assistsant Professor in Theoretical Physics.
He received his PhD from the Bogolyubov Institute for Theoretical Physics in the National Academy
the Norwegian Institute of Science and Technology.
His condensed-matter physics research centers on quantum
the faculty published more than 520 journal articles, or 104 articles per year, with over 8300 literature citations to these articles.
The faculty gave over 250 invited talks, with 150 at professional conferences and workshops, in the past 5 years.
We have seven Fellows of the American Physical Society (APS) and two Fellows of the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS).
Our service courses are in high demand from across campus, with approximately 4000 students enrolled annually in our courses.
If you would like a copy of our 5-year assessment, please contact me or the department office.
In the fall of 2024, our graduate enrollment achieved an all-time high of 65 graduate students.
In the last two years, support of research assistantships by faculty has increased 30%, commensurate with increased
Our faculty have had important professional achievements over the past year. Prof. Haojing Yan was
to full professor. Prof. Xiaoqin Zou was recognized by the Board of Curators as Curators’ Distinguished Professor.
Prof. Carlos Wexler was appointed as the co-Director of the MU Materials Science and Engineering
to faculty in our department and others to promote collaborative research.
Many of our students have had noteworthy achievements over the past year, in fact, too many to highlight
here so please read about those in the following pages and I’m sure you will join us in being very proud of both our graduate and undergraduate students.

Our planet experiences two eclipse seasons annually when the Moon’s orbital path crosses directly between Earth and the Sun. Our location afforded us viewing and outreaching opportunities for both most recent eclipses, with a 60% partial view of last November’s (2023)
totality experience for the April 8, 2024, total eclipse.
viewing stations on Lowry Mall with telescopes and eclipse glasses during each event.
crowded Lowry Mall. Photo credit – Paul Miceli
April graduate students Gourab Nandi, Manoj Gadtoula, and Jenna Bologa,
students ran the event with telescopes and demonstrations at Lowry Mall and interacted with about 750 visitors.
Two groups of our graduate students traveled to the path of totality in southern Missouri on April 8. Charles Mentzer, Alec Martin, Bangzheng Sun, and Bo Yang set up telescopes and provided
Prof. Haojing Yan adjusts the telescope
Qi (graduate student in the School of
Event organizer Teja Teppala and students in
Our Department of Physics and Astronomy held the annual Open House event on Saturday,
April 20, 2024. The event was led by
Yicheng Guo, and three graduate students Teja Teppala,
Chiaro. Fifteen additional volunteers (7 graduate students from PAGSA and 8 undergraduate

150 adults and children. Participants explored more than 50 hands-on displays of Physics and Astronomy, built kaleidoscopes, and made color subtraction pictures. They watched eye- opening large scale physics demonstrations as well as fun demonstrations using liquid nitrogen, enjoying ice cream made on the spot.
floor showed up in the view.

Laws Observatory on top of the Physics Building continues to be used for outreach and education on a weekly basis. Wednesday night viewing for the public is open (weather permitting) all year except during
a mid-summer break. Upgrades to some
There are many other activities for special events. The observatory provided live streaming on Facebook during the
April 8 solar eclipse. The events are run by student volunteers and by Valentine Germann and Randall Durk of the Central Missouri Astronomical Society.
For news and information follow Laws Observatory on Facebook.
joined our department this fall as an assistant professor,
physics at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville. He has a bachelor's degree in engineering physics and a master's degree in fundamental physics from Chalmers
received his physics PhD in 2018 from the University of Texas at
Prof. Gregory Fiete. From graduation until 2021, he was a postdoc at
Oak Ridge National Laboratory, where he developed strong collaborations with quantum magnetism experimentalists, and a deep

joined our department as an assistant teaching professor, beginning fall semester 2024. Prior to this he was an Assistant Professor of Mathematics and Physics at Stephens College. He earned his B.A. in physics and mathematics from Cornell University. He received his Ph.D. in theoretical physics from the University of Michigan, where he investigated the origins of
He also led the undergraduate physics labs at the University of Michigan, designing lessons and building equipment for hands-on
applying machine learning and artificial intelligence to fight online credit card fraud.

joined the department this fall as an assistant professor from his prior position as Associate Professor, Cosmic Dawn Center, Niels Bohr Institute, University of Copenhagen where he worked on a variety of problems in high- redshift astrophysics, other areas of astronomy, astrostatistics, and machine
Dawn Center, a Danish National Research Foundation Center of Excellence
Technical University. He is a co-PI of BUFFALO (Beyond Ultra-deep
And Legacy Observations), an astronomical survey built around the six Hubble
galactic assembly and clustering and prepare targets for observations with the James Webb Space Telescope. He received an
Astrophysical Sciences from Princeton University, and
has accepted the position of George H. Vineyard Assistant Professor of Theoretical Physics and will officially
at the QuSpin Center of Excellence in the Norwegian University of Science and Technology. He received his PhD from the Bogolyubov
he held a few postdoctoral positions at Western University (Canada), Nordita (Sweden), Yale University, and the University of California, Santa Cruz.
and Weyl semimetals. He is interested in transport properties, collective modes, surface states, superconductivity, and magnetism.
is spending one academic year in the Department as a

awardee hosted by Prof. Carlos Wexler. The Fulbright Scholar-in- Residence Program brings visiting scholars from abroad to U.S. colleges and universities, helping the institutions internationalize their curricula, campuses and surrounding communities, and diversify the educational

Prof. Sapag is one of forty-three Fulbright Scholars-in-Residence, and among 1,000 outstanding foreign faculty and professionals who will teach and pursue research in the United States for the 2024-2025 academic year through the worldwide Fulbright Visiting Scholar Program.
Wexler, professor of physics, and teach seminars regarding international scientific collaborations, while enhancing outreach to Columbia Public School students at Centro Latino.
was chosen as the 2024 recipient of the
and the Optica Advanced Photonics Congress in Quebec City. She has
has recently joined Intel Inc. as a process
the APS March Meeting in Minneapolis, Nanoseries
the early universe using the data of the James Webb Space Telescope.
only 2 billion years old. This result changes people's view that spiral galaxies only appear in large numbers in the recent universe. The first author of the paper, graduate

it in the press conference of the 244th Meeting of the American Astronomical Society in Wisconsin in June, 2024. The paper was then reported widely by worldwide media, including Forbes, MSN.com, Sky & Telescope, etc., potentially reaching 200 million people.

With the help of graduate students (particularly Teja Teppala, Charles Mentzer, Bangzheng Sun, and Gourab Nandi) and other faculty members, Dr. Guo coordinated the efforts of holding viewing parties for two solar eclipses (2023/10
locations in Missouri, accessing more than 2,500
Graduate student Teja Teppala received many
to public engagement, outreach, and educational programs, including:
Society Class of 2024 at the 97th Tap Day.

Ambassador with the North American ALMA Science Center (NAASC), National

represent Mizzou to introduce their research to the
legislators of Missouri in the 2024 Undergraduate Research Day at the Missouri
galaxy-galaxy gravitational lensing system in JWST images to unveil the mysterious dark
Undergraduate Jayden Francois was offered Mizzou’s 2024 Award for Academic
highest honors MU offers based on academic
Students from Guo’s group actively participated in professional conferences. Alec Martin presented his work in several conferences; Vicki Kuhn won 3
place in the graduate competition of the Physical and Mathematical Sciences Division at the Show
place in the undergraduate competition of the same event.

Dr. Guo and Vicki Kuhn were on the organizing committee of the American Physical Society’s Conference for Undergraduate Women in Physics (CUWiP) held at Missouri S&T, Rolla, MO. This three-day regional conference’s goal is to help undergraduate women continue
with the opportunity to experience a professional
about graduate school, and professions in
completed work on a grant in South Africa as a Fulbright Specialist.
of State Fulbright Specialist Program, Katti led nano-
ayurvedic medicine research at the University
Africa from July 2 - Aug. 12. He is recognized as one of the world’s leading experts in green nanotechnology and nanomedicine.
a heartfelt farewell to three excellent and

involved developing and deploying advanced single molecule atomic force microscopy techniques to reveal drug-induced membrane protein conformational dynamics as well as to visualize the mechanism of infections emanating from the
This latter line of inquiry resulted in high- profile
. Dr. Schaefer is currently pursuing a Doctor of Medicine
University in St. Louis, where she obtained a full
to her scientific research prowess, Dr.
Schaefer is an outstanding artist. Some of Dr. Schaefer’s artwork currently hangs in the King Laboratory (feel free to stop by and take a look!) and her portrait of Prof. Dorina

Dr. Dylan Weaver’s research in the King lab focused on fundamental studies of protein-lipid interactions. His main PhD work, which was carried out in close collaboration with Prof. Ioan Kosztin’s group,
protein chain influences the force required to remove the protein from the bilayer.
In fall of 2024 Dr. Weaver started a tenure track
Wisconsin, Eau Claire. All the best to Prof. Weaver as he starts out at his new post!

Elizabeth Conley worked in the King laboratory for several years as an undergraduate researcher. In the course of her research Conley identified several shortcomings of a conventional software package used to analyze atomic force microscope

Frustrated by the limitations of the existing software, Conley developed her own method to overcome the drawbacks. We currently have a manuscript under review (with Conley as first author) describing the details of her new algorithm called “PS
All of these contributions were made possible
and the MU Research Excellence Postdoctoral
work on magnetism in nickel monosilicide was
For the first time, Singh’s research group discovered magnetic order at high temperature in a metal widely used by the electronics
newly discovered magnetism of NiSi in semiconductors may lead to faster computers and computer memory.
Missouri Materials Science and Engineering Institute
(https://msei.missouri.edu/). Also in March, Wexler received the Ann K. Covington
Award for Mentoring. The award recognizes members
In May, Mr. Corey Valleroy, an
undergraduate researcher in Wexler’s lab, received
tinction/) for his senior thesis and paper “
infrared absorption spectrum of single‐wall carbon nanotubes dispersed by sodium dodecyl sulfate in aqueous solution: experiments and molecular dynamics study
Award from the American Physical Society Prairie Section Fall 2023 Meeting. Mr. Valleroy is currently working as an Optical Engineer at the University of Minnesota.

Beginning in August, Prof. Karim Sapag will be spending one academic year at the Department as a Fulbright Scholar-in- Residence awardee hosted by Prof. Wexler. Prof. Wexler chaired the American Physical Society
was promoted to full professor in September 2024.
He has continued leading his team using the data from the James Webb Space Telescope to

found a potential filament structure at redshift of 8.3 (corresponding to 610 million years after the Big Bang), signposted by four "infant" galaxies (less than 30 million years old) crunched into a small volume. These are the youngest (albeit not the earliest) galaxies ever reported in the literature; in other words, we are witnessing the structure in the making. The team has also identified a disk-shape galaxy at redshift of 5.289 (1.1 billion years after the Big Bang), a very early time that the previous theoretical models all said that disk- shape galaxies (i.e., systems dominated by rotation but not random motion) would not have come into being. This galaxy is the second
Guha and Paul Miceli, submitted a proposal to the Mizzou Student Success Enhancement Program resulting in a $100,000 grant for the Reese Optics Laboratory in our
department, along with an additional departmental cost share of $17,196. The proposal focuses on enhancing the functionality

Laboratory, located in room 217 of the Physics Building. The planned upgrades include expanding the space to support more hands-on student experiments, acquiring new instruments for laser and quantum optics, and replacing outdated equipment and computers.

Yu has received a Laser Lab Award from Gentec-EO for his course, Light and Modern Optics. The company has selected 12 institutions from academic organizations worldwide for this award. As part of the award, Gentec-EO will provide a portable photodiode-based
has been recognized by the University of Missouri Board of Curators as a Curators’
professorship is the highest and most prestigious academic rank the Board of Curators
Scholarship, funding him as a University of Missouri/University of the Western Cape exchange student for two months. These scholarships
to one UM System student for study at the University of the Western Cape and one University of
any campus of the University of
Jacques Joubert’s team meeting at the
Professor Joseph Willett, who was a Physics and
Astronomy faculty member from 1965 to 2004, passed away at
and he was an admired teacher. He also was an accomplished jazz musician who was well-known in the community.
More information about Joe is in the newsletter.
The department received a wonderful and precious gift from our recent graduate, Dr. Katherine (Katie) Schaefer, who received

student of Prof. Gavin King. A highly accomplished artist, Katie created an oil painting on canvas of the late Prof. Dorina Kosztin who passed away in January 2023. Katie created a remarkable likeness of Dorina positioned in front of a blackboard,
materials as reference. The framed painting (frame provided by current
Physics Library. We are very grateful for this cherished gift.
holding Katie’s painting of the late Prof. Dorina Kosztin.

There are an array of developments and happenings in the department. Missouri Women in Physics and Astronomy (MoWiPA), led by Prof. Maria Mills, is a new group that was formed to support and connect women faculty and graduate students. In December 2023 the department hosted the

American Physical Society Prairie Section Meeting. Prof. Carlos Wexler organized the meeting, which drew approximately 200 participants and included a well-attended public lecture prior to the meeting. Our annual Physics Leaders Meeting was held October 11and 12 of 2024 where the Distinguished
Award was given to Ronald Boain who had an illustrious career in developing rocket trajectories (including the
Apollo program) and who has been a strong supporter of our department. In

Yicheng Guo, along with many graduate students, hosted our annual open house that attracted 150 participants from the community where they engaged in over 50 hands-on physics and astronomy demonstrations.

April, our graduate students and faculty were heavily involved in outreach for the total solar eclipse. They fanned out to various parts of the state as well as organized a viewing on Lowry Mall where there was a large turn out from the campus community.

Association (PAGSA) remains very active. They and the department continue to organize several social events (picnics, Thanksgiving dinner, and luncheons) during the year. Through astronomy students and the enthusiastic support of volunteers Randall Durk
continues to hold the popular Wednesday night viewings for the public.
institute will be co-led by Matt Maschmann, an associate professor of mechanical and aerospace engineering, and
, a professor of physics in the College of
The MUMSEI is a collaboration between the Department of Physics &
materials, quantum materials and materials-at-extremes. By uniting the sciences and engineering at Mizzou, the institute will create new opportunities for joint projects and funding proposals.

Numerous facilities are open and available to both researchers and industry professionals. Additionally, affiliated faculty develop new interdisciplinary academic programs such as certificates and graduate degrees in materials science and engineering to prepare students to work
faculty designated as core or affiliated faculty with MUSEI. More information is available at
From November 30 –December 2, 2023, the Department hosted the
American Physical Society Prairie Section Meeting. Prof.
chaired the conference. The conference was opened by President Mun Choi and started with a public lecture “The Quantum and the Cosmos” by Edward “Rocky” Kolb
invited and 45 contributed talks, and 36 posters and was attended by almost 200
> 3.5 GPA in physics courses, completion of
research, and a publication or presentation
A Certificate in General Honors from the MU Honors College requires students to complete 24 hours
honors credit and maintain a 3.5 cumulative GPA.
To receive Latin honors from our College of Arts and
least 54 of the student’s last 60 hours at MU and:
Laboratory, Advisor Deepak Singh. Research thesis: Elucidation of the Quantum Magnetic dynamics in semi-classical honeycomb lattice.
Assistant Professor at University of Wisconsin-Eau Claire, Advisor Gavin King. Research Thesis: Probing
Kosztin, Research thesis: Theoretical and computational modeling of the interactions of peptides and proteins with lipid bilayer surfaces.
hybrid halide perovskites: insights into chemical vapor deposited films.
These fellowships, scholarships, and awards are possible because of funds generously
supported by our alumni and faculty.
for the academic year 2023-2024 and summer 2024.
Clifford W. Tompson Scholarship in Physics
Clifford W. Tompson Scholarship in Physics
Abhi Abhijeet, Tony Dorhauer, Manoj Gadtoula, Dilan Gamachchige,
Dayton Simpkins, Kishlay Singh, Carson Ziemke
Newell S. Gingrich Physics and Astronomy Endowment
Mohammed Abousetit, Abeeb Ajibade, Bipin Bhattarai, Randy Burns, Ehsan Faridi, Arash Ghobadi, Vishal Jayswal, Brady
Miles, Gourab Nandi, Moudip Nandi, Ishan Pathak, Avinash Sha, Ryan Smith, Marek Spader, Tom Sun, Bibek Tiwari, Mitch

its alumni and faculty. These funds, established out of a love for physics and support of higher education, have benefited our students for many years. One fund, the
was established several years ago by students in the department who gave generously to honor one of their peers.
Melvin Mora was killed in the line of duty just before his graduation date.
Y. Mora, a Mizzou physics major killed in Iraq during an ambush in the line of duty
To honor him, fellow undergraduate students organized a scholarship fund to help students who share Mora’s love of physics achieve their academic goals in his name. The Melvin

Y. Mora Scholarship Fund is an endowed fund that will provide a scholarship for a deserving physics major every year. Physics students worked on fund-raising projects during the 2004-05 school year and plan to continue to solicit funds during the 2005-06 academic year. The students are committed to seeing Melvin
Y. Mora’s name live on in the MU physics department, and they welcome contributions with gratitude.
the department may contact Dr. Paul Miceli, Professor and Chair, at
have gone above and beyond expectations. It is one of the
major in physics and mechanical engineering from St. Peters,
ground-breaking research. Photo: Sam O’Keefe / University of Missouri.
the study. Photo courtesy Vicki Kuhn.
The published article is “JWST Reveals a Surprisingly High Fraction of Galaxies Being Spiral-like
. Citation: Vicki Kuhn et al 2024 ApJL 968 L15. This project was supported by University of Missouri Research Council grants and the Missouri Space Grant Consortium.
presented their research using the James Webb Space Telescope to examine dark matter at the 2024 University of Missouri
the Research Day. The event gives students an opportunity to showcase their accomplishments and allows elected officials to see firsthand the exciting innovations taking place at MU and other system
, a fifth-year graduate student in the department
Education Award and the Mary Elizabeth Gutermuth Award for Community

School at the 11th Annual Graduate and Postdoctoral Awards. The Sandra K. Abell Science Education Award recognizes the outstanding achievements of a graduate student pursuing PhD studies in any natural science discipline who has demonstrated a dual passion for research and teaching. The Mary Elizabeth Gutermuth Award for Community Engagement supports a graduate student who has a history of and interest in community service through engaged
Teja was tapped into the Rollins Society
Tap Day at the University. Rollins Society is one of the six secret
Day, the Rollins Society inducts eight graduate and professional students
the Society’s watchwords Officium (Duty), Fides (Faithfulness), and Ductus (Leadership).

an undergraduate student pursuing a dual degree in biological sciences and physics, were two of four MU students (seven across the University of Missouri System) selected for the second class of recipients to receive the Remington R. Williams Award from the University of Missouri System recognizing their service outreach at MU. The award is the highest non-academic award bestowed on students by the Board of Curators and is given in memory of Remington Williams and his outstanding service as student representative to the board from 2020 to 2022. Teja serves as the outreach coordinator for the Physics and Astronomy Graduate Student

Association and is co-lead for the Graduate Professional Council’s programming committee. He also is a leader of the College of Agriculture, Food and Natural Resources’ international student group. Vishvi serves as a student ambassador for the Office of Undergraduate Research and is a student representative for the Chancellor’s Committee
for Persons with Disabilities and an undergraduate representative for the Chancellor’s Committee for Libraries.
Physics major and Mizzou Volleyball player Marina
Crownover, Mizzou Football’s Jack Meyer and
Soccer’s Leah Selm in front of the Vehicle Assembly
Building at NASA’s John F. Kennedy Space Center.
Physics major and Mizzou Volleyball athlete

was selected by NASA to participate in a prestigious summer micro- internship at the John F. Kennedy Space Center. NASA selected only seven college students for the internship, with three MU student athletes chosen for the group. Katie Martinez, assistant athletic director for student-athlete career development at Mizzou Athletics assisted in the competitive application process. NASA’s Student Volunteer Experience is a week-long educational program designed for college students who are unable to participate in traditional summer-long internships because of other commitments. Crownover worked
received the 2024 Outstanding Graduate Student Research at the American Conference on Neutron Scattering in Knoxville, TN held June 23 – 27, 2024. David’s advisor is Paul Miceli.
received an American Physical Society Division of Materials
April. Dallar’s advisors are Suchi Guha and Ping Yu.
was selected for the Department of Energy Office of Science
86 graduate students selected nationally for this program. He will study materials sciences and chemistry for microelectronics at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in Tennessee.
Creative Activities Forum poster presentation organized by the MU Graduate Professional Committee in April. Tapesh’s advisor is Sashi Satpathy.
of the year award. This international recognition is from the Microbiology Society.
as a University of Missouri/University of the Western Cape exchange student for two months. Abeeb’s advisor is Xiaoqin Zou.
at the American Physical Society Prairie Section Fall 2023
of Professor Gavin King’s lab, won travel awards to present research at the 2024 Biophysics Society meeting in Philadelphia, PA.
STEM students already in established research programs to enhance their graduate school
plenty of opportunities to relax and connect outside of the lab and classroom.
Our Mentoring Program thrived this year, especially with a larger group of new students joining us.
We now have 18 grad students actively participating, helping new members navigate
amazing to see these supportive relationships blossom. The journal clubs,
also been a highlight, offering a supportive environment for practicing
PAGSA also made a significant impact through outreach
April 8th, we coordinated an eclipse event in collaboration with MU Extension, setting up telescopes and handing out eclipse
one on the quirky Ig Nobel Prize winners!
Nandi (right) looks on at Lowry Mall.
Right photo: over 750 people attended the event. Photos
a success. We’re looking forward to more great moments with PAGSA in the coming year!

within the Physics Department, to foster connections with its members that will last beyond their time here, and to enhance the academic pursuits of the women in the department. It has been an exciting
we applied for and received $1000 from an
Women in Physics Grant to help fund some of our activities
kicked off with our annual Liquid
of events throughout the year. These included student research presentations, faculty talks, and fun trivia
nights. We also organized sessions on general physics topics, graduate school and REU applications, and the latest
was our graduate student panel, where undergraduates had
the opportunity to ask questions about life in graduate
Argonne National Laboratory: Front row: Hunter
Lucker, Mathieux Harper, Max Sanson, Gracye
Argonne National Laboratory: Front row: Hunter
Lucker, Mathieux Harper, Max Sanson, Gracye
Aquarium: Front row: Hunter Lucker, Patrick
In January, we took a trip to Chicago, IL.
the Museum of Science and Industry, Fermilab, Adler Planetarium, and Millennium Park. We look forward to a similar trip this year!
We focused on growing our membership, both within the physics department and outside of it. This year, we have about 20 active members, maintaining our levels from last year and
about half of our members are physics majors and the other half are from
included a trip to Laws Observatory, astronomy research presentation (with an emphasis on how to get involved in research and apply for REUs), a space movie night, astronomy trivia, and, of
various events, we try to emphasize outreach to the general public and encourage students to get involved in scientific research.
The highlight of the past year was our annual stargazing spring break trip. This year 8 Mizzou students
our stay, we still got to hike, explore the river, and talk to a park ranger about astronomy, since the park is an International Dark Sky

Area. Luckily, on the last night of the trip, a few of our members got a glimpse of some beautiful stars. We plan to continue the spring break tradition this year with a trip to a different dark sky area!
adding new members, and any alumni can attend the event.

general outline for the fall meetings includes briefings from Department Chair, Prof. Paul Miceli, Director of Graduate Students Prof. Suchi Guha, and Director of Undergraduate Studies Prof. Silvia Bompadre. Our students provide updates on our student organizations. The Dean of
A&S Cooper Drury drops by to talk about
A&S topics and answer questions from the alumni. There is an opportunity for a panel
As part of their giving back to the department, Physics Leaders evaluate and award first, second, and sometimes third place monetary prizes to selected student presenters.
Friday evening event with socializing and dinner is held where these student awards, faculty recognition awards, and a department Distinguished Alumni are announced and awarded.
other department briefings, students Julianna Bayless talked about Society of
Physics Students activities and Vicki Kuhn summarized PAGSA (Physics
Award for best doctoral dissertation to Dr. Katherine
was selected as the recipient of the Distinguished Alumni
Paul Miceli presented the award via Zoom.

founding and long-time member of Physics Leaders, Dr. Seeser was recognized not only for his career and leader/department contributions, but also for his work as a volunteer and philanthropist for numerous organizations including the Central Institute for the Deaf, the Washington University Program in
Audiology and Communications Sciences program, FIRST® (For Inspiration and Recognition of Science and
President John Shumway presented 2023 Physics Leader’s
Awards for the Friday afternoon student presentations. Outstanding
Outstanding Graduate Student Presentation Awards went to 1st place - Dallar
Awards to Silvia Bompadre, Haojing Yan, Suchi Guha, and Ioan Kosztin.
The 2024 Physics Leaders meeting was held October 11-12. In addition to the annual “state of the department” briefings, PAGSA
year and SPS President Julianna Bayless updated the leaders on undergraduate activities. Chair Paul Miceli along with Ron Boain presented the Boain Dissertation
place) over Zoom as they could not attend in person.
Faculty, students and alumni again enjoyed
Vann Priest presented the Outstanding Student Presentation Awards to undergraduates
Evan Restuccia and Donovan Flagg, who tied for 1
in a Zoom call with Randy
Gavin King, Aigen Li, Deepak Singh, Carlos Wexler, and Ping Yu.
Distinguished Alumni Award, presented to Ronald Boain. Ron was recognized for his long and
and JPL on many projects, such as optimizing trajectory designs for spacecraft, and working in critical programs such as CloudSat, for which he received NASA’s Exceptional
Ron and his wife Catherine have also invested in our students through the Ronald J. Boain and Catherine J. Rangel Boain Endowment fund, the annual Boain PhD Dissertation
Award in Physics, and the Boain PhD Student Travel Fund.
of Columbia, Missouri passed away at the age of 94 on November 6, 2023. He was born in
Albany, Missouri on June 9, 1929, and completed his undergraduate and graduate work at the University
1956, working with Bernard Goodman in the area of quantum statistical mechanics.
After working in governmental and industrial research positions
a professor where he taught in the Department of Physics and Astronomy for over 40 years. His research in nuclear fusion
physicist by day and loved to teach graduate and undergraduate students, he was a jazz musician by night and organized his first band at the age of 13.
Willett was a talented piano player, but tenor sax and clarinet were his passion. Well-
venues all over Columbia for over 50 years and was considered to be one of the best clarinet players of his era. Dr. Willett retired from the University in
